frame1_4.apiKeyExposed: API key exposed in public repository
frame1_4.exposureMessage: {{hours}} hours of exposure. Who has the key?
frame1_4.botsNote: Bots scan GitHub every second for leaked secrets.

frame1_6.title: Incident Response Checklist
frame1_6.attackersNote: Meanwhile, attackers have had the key for 10+ hours...

frame1_7.title: Every Secret is a Liability
frame1_7.requirements: Each secret requires: storage • access control • rotation • auditing

frame1_8.title: Problem: Knowing the secret = Being the identity
frame1_8.subtitle: We're using shared secrets as identity.
frame1_8.conclusion: Anyone with the secret can pretend to be anyone.

frame1_10.fullName: Secure Production Identity Framework for Everyone

frame2_1.fullName: Secure Production Identity Framework for Everyone
frame2_1.notAProduct: Not a product—a specification anyone can implement.

frame2_2.title: Three Core Ideas
frame2_2.pillar2Description: A way to prove that name
frame2_2.together: Together, these enable secure workload identity.
frame2_2.pillar2DescriptionLine1: A way to prove that name

frame2_3.title: The SPIFFE ID
frame2_3.label: SPIFFE ID
frame2_3.description: A unique, permanent name in URI format

frame2_4.title: SPIFFE ID Anatomy
frame2_4.description: The SPIFFE ID is a URI that uniquely identifies a workload within its trust domain.
frame2_4.analogy: Like an email address for services: user@company.com
frame2_4.descriptionLine1: The SPIFFE ID is a URI that uniquely
frame2_4.descriptionLine2: identifies a workload within its trust domain.

frame2_5.title: Real-World Examples
frame2_5.pathNote: The path structure is flexible—define it to match your organization.

frame2_6.title: Trust Domains: The Boundary
frame2_6.description1: A trust domain is a zone of trust
frame2_6.description2: managed by a single identity authority

frame2_7.title: Think of it Like a Company
frame2_7.insideShared: Everyone inside shares the same badge system
frame2_7.issuedBy: issued by the same security authority

frame2_8.title: Why Trust Domains Matter
frame2_8.federationNote: Federation = explicitly agreeing to trust another domain's identities

frame2_9.title: The Badge Metaphor
frame2_9.spiffeIdExplain: Your SPIFFE ID is your identity (permanent).
frame2_9.svidExplain: Your SVID is proof of that identity (temporary).
frame2_9.note: We'll use this metaphor throughout—it maps surprisingly well.

frame2_10.title: SVID: Your Identity Document
frame2_10.summary: The physical badge that proves your identity

frame2_11.title: SVIDs Are Short-Lived
frame2_11.blastRadius: Short lifetimes = limited blast radius if compromised

frame2_13.title: Metaphor Limits
frame2_13.intro: Like all analogies, this one has limits
frame2_13.remember: The concepts map well, but remember:
frame2_13.machineSpeed: SPIFFE operates at machine speed with cryptographic proofs.

frame3_1.title: SPIFFE Needs an Implementation
frame3_1.conclusion: SPIRE is the reference implementation of SPIFFE

frame3_3.title: The SPIRE Server
frame3_3.metaphor: Think of it like corporate headquarters—
frame3_3.metaphorConclusion: the central authority that decides who gets an official badge

frame3_4.title: Server Responsibilities
frame3_4.conclusion: The Server is the brain of the operation—it knows who should get which identity.

frame3_5.title: The SPIRE Agent
frame3_5.metaphor: The Agent runs on every node—
frame3_5.metaphorConclusion: issuing badges to local workloads on behalf of headquarters

frame3_6.title: Agent Responsibilities
frame3_6.conclusion: The Agent is the local badge printer—always available, always fast.

frame3_7.title: The Architecture
frame3_7.conclusion: Together they form a distributed identity system.

frame3_8.title: But Wait: Who Gets Identity?
frame3_8.answer: It needs a mapping: workload properties → SPIFFE ID

frame3_9.title: Registration Entries
frame3_9.subtitle: The HR records of the identity system
frame3_9.description: Registration entries map selectors (workload properties) to SPIFFE IDs (identity)

frame3_10.title: Selectors: The Criteria
frame3_10.description: Selectors describe workload properties the Agent can verify
frame3_10.conclusion: Different platforms expose different properties—selectors are extensible.

frame3_11.title: Entry Example
frame3_11.rule1: Only workloads matching ALL selectors get the identity.
frame3_11.rule2: This is how SPIRE enforces "who gets what."

frame3_12.title: The Full Picture
frame3_12.step1: Admin creates registration entry in Server

frame4_1.title: The Bootstrap Problem
frame4_1.before: Before the Agent can issue badges to workloads,
frame4_1.beforeConclusion: the Server needs to trust the Agent itself.

frame4_2.title: Traditional vs SPIFFE Approach
frame4_2.traditionalProblem: Problem: Secrets can be stolen and used by attackers
frame4_2.spiffeProblem: Can't be faked without compromising the platform
frame4_2.keyInsightLine1: The Agent doesn't claim its own identity—
frame4_2.keyInsightLine2: the underlying platform vouches for it.

frame4_3.title: Node Attestation
frame4_3.definition: The process by which a SPIRE Agent proves it's running on a legitimate node
frame4_3.provides: The platform provides cryptographic proof that:

frame4_4.title: The Badge Metaphor
frame4_4.analogyText: City Hall certifies that a building is real. The badge printer inside is then trusted to issue badges to employees in that building.

frame4_5.title: Platform as Witness
frame4_5.platformSays: "I vouch for this Agent—it's running on my infrastructure"
frame4_5.agentDoesNotClaim: The Agent doesn't claim its own identity
frame4_5.platformProvides: The platform provides signed proof that:
frame4_5.proof2: the Agent is running on it

frame4_6.title: How Node Attestation Works
frame4_6.step4: Agent sends proof to SPIRE Server
frame4_6.step5: Server verifies proof with platform API
frame4_6.complete: ✓ Agent is now attested and can issue workload SVIDs

frame4_7.title: Attestation Types
frame4_7.conclusion: SPIRE's plugin architecture supports many more attestation methods.

frame4_8.title: Platform Deep Dive
frame4_8.explorationText: Each platform has unique attestation details worth exploring:
frame4_8.bullet1: AWS: Instance Identity Documents + IID signature
frame4_8.bullet3: GCP: Instance identity tokens from metadata server
frame4_8.pattern: Platform cryptographically vouches for the node → Agent is trusted → Agent can issue SVIDs

frame4_9.title: After Attestation
frame4_9.agentSVIDNote: The Agent's own SVID proves it's authorized to speak for this node.
frame4_9.next: Next: How workloads get their identities...

frame4_10.title: Why Attackers Can't Spoof
frame4_10.attackerSays: I'll pretend to be a trusted Agent!
frame4_10.explanation1: The platform (AWS, K8s, etc.) is the source of truth
frame4_10.explanation3: Without valid proof, the Server rejects the Agent

frame4_11.title: Try It: Attestation Simulator
frame4_11.subtitle: Explore how node attestation works in different scenarios

frame5_1.title: Node Trusted. Now What?
frame5_1.workloadsNeed: Workloads on this node need their own identities

frame5_2.title: Workload Attestation
frame5_2.description: Agent examines workload to decide if it matches a registration entry

frame5_3.title: The Badge Metaphor
frame5_3.metaphorText: HR verifies employment before printing badge

frame5_4.title: Developer Experience
frame5_4.callWorkloadAPI: Call the Workload API, get your identity
frame5_4.noCredentials: No credentials needed. No secrets to manage.

frame5_5.title: The Workload API
frame5_5.workloadsConnect: Workloads just connect to the socket
frame5_5.agentUses: The Agent uses the connection itself

frame5_6.title: How the Agent Decides
frame5_6.examines: Agent examines: What process? What user?

frame5_8.title: The Magic Moment
frame5_8.note: Without ever providing a secret to get it!

frame5_10.title: But Wait—Is There a Secret?
frame5_10.doesntSVID: Doesn't the SVID have a private key?
frame5_10.explanation: The private key never leaves the workload—it's generated locally and never transmitted.

frame5_11.title: Why It's Different
frame5_11.conclusion: No human ever sees it. No human ever manages it.

frame5_12.title: Quiz: Selector Matcher
frame5_12.subtitle: Match workloads to their registration entries based on selectors

frame6_1.title: You Have Identity. Now What?
frame6_1.question: How do I securely communicate with other services?

frame6_2.title: mTLS: Mutual Authentication
frame6_2.bothSides: Both sides prove who they are
frame6_2.notJustServer: Not just the server—the client too!

frame6_3.title: The Badge Metaphor
frame6_3.metaphorText: Both show their badges before discussing anything sensitive

frame6_5.title: Trust Bundle: The Verifier
frame6_5.question: How does a workload know to trust another's SVID?
frame6_5.publicKeys: Public keys of trusted Certificate Authorities
frame6_5.explanation: If the SVID was signed by a CA in my trust bundle,

frame6_6.title: The Badge Metaphor
frame6_6.metaphorText: The trust bundle is like the list of companies whose badges your card readers accept

frame6_7.title: Authentication ≠ Authorization
frame6_7.conclusion: Authorization is handled separately by your application or policies
frame6_7.authNExplanation: (proving who you are), not AUTHORIZATION (what you can do)

frame6_8.title: The Badge Metaphor
frame6_8.badgeProves: Your badge proves WHO you are
frame6_8.policyDecides: The access list decides WHAT you can do
frame6_8.badgeProvesEmployee: Your badge proves you're an employee
frame6_8.separatePolicy: It doesn't determine which rooms you can enter—that's a separate policy

frame6_9.title: Authorization Layers
frame6_9.conclusion: SPIFFE tells you WHO—authorization policies tell you WHAT

frame6_10.title: Try It: mTLS Handshake
frame6_10.subtitle: Step through a complete mutual TLS handshake between workloads

frame6_11.title: Zero Trust Foundation
frame6_11.spiffeProvides: SPIFFE provides the identity foundation for zero trust
frame6_11.conclusion: Verify explicitly. Never assume trust based on network location.

frame7_1.title: SVIDs Don't Last Forever
frame7_1.duration: Minutes to hours, not months or years

frame7_2.title: Why So Short?
frame7_2.compromised: A compromised SVID is only useful briefly

frame7_3.title: Rotation Is Automatic
frame7_3.description: Before expiration, Agent fetches fresh SVIDs

frame7_4.title: The Badge Metaphor
frame7_4.autoRenew: Your badge auto-renews every hour. You never think about it.

frame7_5.title: The Overlap Period
frame7_5.description: New SVIDs issued before old ones expire

frame7_7.title: Why Stability Matters
frame7_7.policiesReference: Policies reference SPIFFE IDs, not SVIDs
frame7_7.svidRotates: SVID rotates: v1 → v2 → v3 → v4...

frame7_9.title: Try It: Rotation Simulator
frame7_9.subtitle: Watch SVIDs rotate while the SPIFFE ID stays constant

frame7_10.title: Lifecycle Complete
frame7_10.automatic: All automatic. All cryptographic. No human intervention.

frame8_1.title: Beyond Single Domains
frame8_1.question: How do different trust domains communicate?

frame8_2.title: Federation
frame8_2.description: Domains exchange trust bundles to verify each other's SVIDs

frame8_3.title: The Badge Metaphor
frame8_3.acmeQuote: "We'll accept badges from Partner Corp at our facilities"

frame8_4.title: How Federation Works
frame8_4.step2: 2. Each domain learns to verify the other's SVIDs

frame8_5.title: Federation Topologies
frame8_5.chooseBasedOn: Choose based on your trust relationships

frame8_6.title: Platform Attestation: AWS
frame8_6.conclusion: AWS cryptographically vouches for the instance identity

frame8_7.title: Platform Attestation: Kubernetes
frame8_7.conclusion: Kubernetes vouches for pod identity via projected tokens

frame8_8.title: Platform Attestation: GCP/Azure
frame8_8.commonPatternDescription: Platform provides cryptographic proof of instance identity
frame8_8.conclusion: Each cloud has native attestation—SPIRE supports them all

frame8_9.title: Platform Attestation: TPM
frame8_9.conclusion: Proves the physical machine's identity—strongest attestation

frame8_11.title: OIDC Federation
frame8_11.bridgeText: Bridge workload identity to cloud IAM
frame8_11.conclusion: Access cloud resources using your SPIFFE identity

frame9_1.title: What We've Learned
frame9_1.conclusion: The complete identity infrastructure for modern workloads

frame9_2.title: The Badge Summary
frame9_2.conclusion: The Agent handles everything—you just work

frame9_4.fromZeroToExpert: From zero to workload identity expert in {{totalFrames}} frames.

attestationSimulator.success_step0_desc: SPIRE Agent launches on the node

selectorMatcher.perfectScore: Perfect! You understand workload attestation selectors.
selectorMatcher.partialScore: Selectors must match ALL properties for attestation to succeed.
selectorMatcher.q1_explanation: Entry A matches both selectors: namespace "payments" AND service account "payment-service"
selectorMatcher.q2_explanation: Entry B matches both: Docker label "app=cache" AND Unix UID 1000
selectorMatcher.q3_explanation: Entry B requires both namespace AND the correct pod label. Entry A is too permissive!

rotationSimulator.keyInsight: 💡 The SPIFFE ID never changes—only the SVID rotates automatically!

e2eSimulator.success_step0_desc: Client wants to talk to Server. Both have SVIDs.
e2eSimulator.success_step1_desc: Client presents its SVID certificate to Server.
e2eSimulator.success_step2_desc: Server presents its SVID certificate to Client.
e2eSimulator.success_step3_desc: Client checks Server SVID against its Trust Bundle.
e2eSimulator.success_step4_desc: Server checks Client SVID against its Trust Bundle.
e2eSimulator.success_step5_desc: Both sides verified. Encrypted channel ready.
e2eSimulator.failure_step0_desc: Client wants to talk to Server. But is the Server trusted?
e2eSimulator.failure_step1_desc: Client presents its SVID certificate to Server.
e2eSimulator.failure_step2_desc: Server presents its SVID certificate to Client.
e2eSimulator.failure_step3_desc: Server's CA is not in Client's Trust Bundle!
